coredata.dc:description: Latent heat storage (LHS) systems are essential for advancing low-emission, high-efficiency energy technologies. However, the low thermal conductivity of phase change materials (PCMs) limits their performance, motivating the development of effective heat-transfer enhancement strategies. This study evaluates the synergistic influence of fin configuration and shell geometry on the melting efficiency and heat transfer characteristics of shell-and-tube LHS units. Six shell-and-tube heat exchangers (HXs) with rectangular and bio-inspired fins, each with constant fin and PCM volume, were fabricated using obround and circular shells. Experimental and numerical analyses were conducted to evaluate key thermal performance metrics. The findings revealed that the integration of the tree-shaped fin configuration and the obround shell geometry resulted in the most pronounced enhancement in melting performance and heat transfer rate. Compared to conventional rectangular fins, tree-shaped fins reduced the melting time by 13 % and 10 % in the obround and circular shells, respectively, and increased the time-averaged heat transfer rates by 12 % and 10 %. Modifying the shell geometry from circular to obround reduced the melting time by 40 %, 42 %, and 42 % and increased the time-averaged heat-transfer rate by 58 %, 55 %, and 61 % for the rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped fin configurations, respectively. A further study of fin dimensions showed that reducing fin thickness decreased melting time by up to 74 % in the circular shell and 41 % in the obround shell, both using tree-shaped fin configurations.

head.ce:title: Bio-inspired fins and obround shell geometry for enhanced shell-and-tube latent heat storage: An experimental and numerical study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Latent heat storage (LHS) systems are essential for advancing low-emission, high-efficiency energy technologies. However, the low thermal conductivity of phase change materials (PCMs) limits their performance, motivating the development of effective heat-transfer enhancement strategies. This study evaluates the synergistic influence of fin configuration and shell geometry on the melting efficiency and heat transfer characteristics of shell-and-tube LHS units. Six shell-and-tube heat exchangers (HXs) with rectangular and bio-inspired fins, each with constant fin and PCM volume, were fabricated using obround and circular shells. Experimental and numerical analyses were conducted to evaluate key thermal performance metrics. The findings revealed that the integration of the tree-shaped fin configuration and the obround shell geometry resulted in the most pronounced enhancement in melting performance and heat transfer rate. Compared to conventional rectangular fins, tree-shaped fins reduced the melting time by 13 % and 10 % in the obround and circular shells, respectively, and increased the time-averaged heat transfer rates by 12 % and 10 %. Modifying the shell geometry from circular to obround reduced the melting time by 40 %, 42 %, and 42 % and increased the time-averaged heat-transfer rate by 58 %, 55 %, and 61 % for the rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped fin configurations, respectively. A further study of fin dimensions showed that reducing fin thickness decreased melting time by up to 74 % in the circular shell and 41 % in the obround shell, both using tree-shaped fin configurations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: To address the global energy crisis and promote sustainable economic and social development, two key strategies are essential: improving the efficiency of conventional energy systems and balancing the supply and demand of renewable energy sources [1,2]. Renewable sources, such as solar, tidal power, and wind, have garnered significant interest due to their sustainability, abundance, and accessibility. Nevertheless, their inherently intermittent and variable behavior, greatly impacted by geographical location, seasonal changes, and unpredictable weather patterns, poses challenges for widespread adoption. To overcome these limitations, energy storage systems provide a crucial solution by enabling the storage of excess energy during periods of abundance and its release during times of high demand [3–5]. Energy storage technologies are classified into four major types depending on their storage mechanisms, including thermal, electromagnetic, mechanical, and chemical. Among these, thermal energy storage is particularly important and can be categorized into three main types: thermochemical, sensible, and latent heat storage (LHS). LHS, using phase change materials (PCMs), is a favorable approach for thermal energy storage owing to its near-isothermal operation and high energy storage density [6].

PCMs have a broad range of applications across diverse sectors, including solar thermal energy systems [7], building energy efficiency [8,9], battery thermal management [10,11], air conditioning [12], waste heat recovery [13], and electronics [14,15]. However, their inherently low thermal conductivity significantly limits the efficiency of heat transfer and overall energy storage performance. To address this issue, various strategies have been investigated to enhance heat transfer [16]. These approaches include the insertion of fins [17–25], incorporation of heat pipes [14,26], dispersing nanoparticles [27–29], adding carbon-based materials [18,30,31], utilization of rotating heat exchangers (HXs) [32–34], encapsulation techniques [35], employing multiple PCMs [36,37], close-contact melting [38,39], adjusting enclosure inclination [40–42], bioinspired materials and structures [43,44], and integration of high-conductivity metal foam and wire mesh [45–47]. Furthermore, the design of shell geometry and the arrangement of the heat transfer fluid (HTF) tube significantly influence the thermal behavior of LHS units [48–54]. Among these approaches, using fins is a well-known and efficient method for enhancing heat transfer due to its simplicity, ease of manufacturing, reliability, and cost-effectiveness. Conventional fins are typically categorized as longitudinal [55], pin [56], annular [57], and spiral [58], based on their spatial arrangement. Although these fins improve heat transfer to the adjacent PCM, their limited spatial coverage results in non-uniform thermal distribution. Consequently, PCM near the fins undergoes phase change more rapidly than regions farther away, leading to uneven melting and solidification within the storage domain. To overcome non-uniform heat distribution in conventional LHS systems, recent studies have explored complex fin configurations inspired by naturally efficient, tree-like branching patterns found in plant roots, leaf veins, river networks, and respiratory systems (Fig. 1 ). These bio-inspired designs aim to improve thermal spreading within the PCM by enhancing both conductive and convective heat transfer. Several fin geometries, such as snowflake-shaped [59], Y-shaped [60], and triangular [61] fins, have been proposed to enhance heat transfer pathways.

Recent studies have increasingly focused on the development and evaluation of bio-inspired fin configurations to enhance thermal performance in LHS systems. These investigations aim to improve melting and solidification behavior by optimizing heat transfer pathways through conductive and convective mechanisms. Huang et al. [20] utilized both experimental measurements and computational modeling to compare the performance of uniform and gradient tree-shaped fin designs in horizontal LHS units. Their findings showed that gradient tree-shaped fins improved melting performance relative to uniform fin layout, reducing the melting time duration by 9 % and enhancing temperature uniformity. However, this design increased discharge time by 57.4 %. Wang et al. [62] numerically simulated the effect of perforations on a tree-shaped fin design. Their study demonstrated that, compared to rectangular fins, one, two, and three layers of perforations reduced discharge times by 52.76 %, 52.56 %, and 51.99 %, respectively. Furthermore, relative to solid tree-shaped fins, one- and two-layer perforated fins reduced solidification times by 1.14 % and 0.71 %, respectively, whereas three-layer perforated fins increased solidification time by 0.46 %. Zhang et al. [1] numerically demonstrated that a fractal tree-shaped fin can noticeably enhance both the energy discharge rate and temperature uniformity in LHS systems. Their results indicated that, compared to a radial-fin design, the optimized tree-fin design achieved reductions of 66.2 % and 4.4 % in solidification and melting time, respectively. Wang et al. [63] numerically investigated a lobed triplex-tube HX for solar thermal energy storage. The results revealed that combining a lobed geometry with Y-shaped fins within the PCM annulus substantially improved the melting rate, reducing the total melting time by approximately 31.8 % and increasing the average heat absorption rate by 48.39 % compared to a simple triplex-tube HX. Jiang et al. [4] numerically showed that a bio-inspired leaf-like fin design significantly outperformed conventional designs, achieving a 41.6 % reduction in complete solidification time and a 69.9 % increase in average discharge power. Safari et al. [64] conducted combined experimental and numerical studies to evaluate the impact of different bifurcated fin arrangements on the melting performance of a LHS heat exchanger. The results showed that fin arrangement significantly affects thermal performance. For shorter fin lengths, the configuration with bifurcated fins concentrated in the lower half of the enclosure achieved the greatest melting time reduction, improving performance by 19.5 % compared to the straight-fin baseline. For longer fin lengths, the bifurcated design with a diagonal cross-fin arrangement proved most effective, resulting in a maximum melting time reduction of 61.6 %. In an investigation by Shi et al. [21], the heat transfer performance of a vertical triple-tube HX equipped with fractal fins was evaluated against that of a conventional rectangular fin design. The results demonstrated that the fractal fins enhanced thermal performance, reducing the PCM melting time by 27.3 % compared to the rectangular configuration. In a numerical analysis, Hong et al. [65] evaluated the thermal efficiency of a novel twin-branched snow crystal fin design in a concentric LHS system. The results revealed that triple snow crystal fins, compared to rectangular fins, reduced melting time by up to 82.8 %, while quadruple snow crystal fins reduced solidification time by 66.4 %.

As highlighted in the literature, numerous studies have examined the influence of fin configurations on the thermal performance of PCMs. However, most of this research has focused on HXs with circular shell geometries. In previous work, the authors demonstrated that a shell-and-tube HX with an obround shell geometry outperformed its circular counterpart in terms of melting performance [49]. Despite this advantage, the application of bio-inspired fins in non-circular enclosures remains largely unexplored, representing a significant gap in the understanding of how the combined effects of fin configurations and shell geometry influence the thermal behavior of PCM-based systems. Moreover, the majority of the existing literature relies predominantly on numerical simulations, underscoring the critical need for experimental validation. To address these issues and advance the field, this study experimentally visualizes the combined influence of fin configurations and shell geometries on the thermal performance of LHS systems. Notably, this study is the first to investigate the melting behavior and thermal performance of PCM in obround HXs incorporating bio-inspired finned tubes. In this study, HXs incorporating rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped fins were examined in both obround and circular shell geometries while maintaining a constant volume of fins and PCM across all configurations. The circular-shell HX with rectangular fins serves as the baseline for comparative analysis, establishing a benchmark for evaluating the effectiveness of the different fin configurations and shell geometries. Rigorous experimental measurements and validated numerical models were employed to analyze the development of the melting interface, heat transfer rate, and temperature profiles in the LHS systems.

The HTF circulation system, designed to transfer heat from water to the PCM, comprised two concentric copper tubes, as depicted in Fig. 4 and detailed in our previous work [49]. Hot water at an inlet temperature of 85 °C enters the HX through the inner tube inlet at a mass flow rate of approximately 0.1 kg/s, corresponding to a Reynolds number of 12,172. The configuration used for the nested tubes, as well as the high flow rate of the HTF, results in a negligible temperature difference between the inlet and outlet of the HTF. The small temperature variation, combined with the high thermal conductivity and thin wall of the copper tube, ensures a nearly uniform surface temperature, leading to a consistent melting pattern along the length of the HX. To assess the impact of fin design on the melting process, three distinct copper fin configurations, rectangular [66], Y-shaped, and tree-shaped, were investigated. For each configuration, four copper fins were equally spaced and welded to the HTF tube, maintaining a constant total volume of fin for all designs. Photographs of the fin configurations employed in this study are presented in Fig. 5 , and the detailed dimensional specifications of the fins are provided in Fig. 6 and Table 1 . Four K-type thermocouples were positioned at distinct radial locations on a single mid-plane cross-section of the HX to minimize flow disturbance. Owing to the uniform temperature distribution along the tube axis and the symmetric fin arrangement, the convective flow within the HX can be considered two-dimensional, with similar temperature patterns on both sides of the vertical y-axis. Therefore, the thermocouples were installed only on the left-hand side of the HX, as illustrated in Fig. 6. The thermocouples were connected to channels of a data acquisition multiplexer, and temperature data were recorded every two seconds. Paraffin with 99 % purity supplied by Sigma-Aldrich was selected as the PCM because of its favorable thermal properties. The physical and thermal properties of the paraffin and copper used in this investigation were identical to those reported in our earlier work [49].

To support the experimental investigation, a computational model was developed to evaluate the effect of fin configurations on the melting performance of PCM within circular and obround shell geometries. The model replicated the exact geometrical dimensions of the experimental LHS units, including the fins, enclosures, and HTF tubes. To reduce computational time without compromising accuracy, a two-dimensional half-domain model was employed, leveraging the system's symmetry along the vertical mid-plane. A non-uniform quadrilateral mesh was employed, with higher mesh resolution near heat transfer surfaces to capture steep thermal gradients.

The molten PCM was modeled as an incompressible, Newtonian fluid. Its physical and thermal properties were assumed to be constant throughout the melting process. Heat losses to the ambient, radiative heat transfer, viscous dissipation, and thermal contact resistance were neglected. These assumptions are consistent with established literature and serve to simplify the computational analysis while preserving the fidelity of the phase change behavior. The melting process of the PCM was simulated using the enthalpy–porosity method, which is widely adopted for modeling phase change phenomena coupled with natural convection [67]. The governing equations include the continuity, momentum, and energy equations, as follows:

Initially, the PCM domain was set to a uniform temperature of 25 °C. The HTF tube wall was maintained at a constant temperature of 85 °C to drive the melting process. The outer surface of the HX was assumed adiabatic to minimize heat loss, expressed as ∇T = 0. No-slip velocity boundary conditions (
u →
= 0) were applied on all solid surfaces in contact with the PCM. Fig. 7 illustrates the boundary and initial conditions implemented in the simulation.

The governing equations, along with the boundary and initial conditions, were discretized employing a control volume method on a staggered grid. The pressure-velocity coupling was solved using a coupled algorithm. A second-order upwind discretization scheme was used for the momentum and energy equations to ensure numerical stability. The under-relaxation factors for energy, pressure correction, and momentum were set to 0.5, 0.4, and 0.3, respectively. The pressure correction equation was solved employing the PRESTO! scheme. The iterative calculation process was terminated when the residuals of the governing equations were less than 10−6, ensuring a satisfactory level of convergence in the solution. A first-order implicit time-step discretization scheme was adopted to achieve a stable solution for all simulations. A comprehensive grid and time-step independence study was conducted for all geometrical configurations to ensure that the numerical results were not influenced by the number of mesh elements or the time-step size. For the circular-shell HXs, grid resolutions of 50,256, 54,682, and 59,478 elements were adopted for the rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped fin designs, respectively. For the obround shell geometries, the corresponding grid resolutions were 55,520, 57,182, and 61,156 elements. In all cases, a uniform time step of 0.1 s was found to ensure numerical stability and accurate prediction of phase change dynamics. Furthermore, the influence of the mushy zone parameter (A mushy) on the numerical results was evaluated using values ranging from 5 × 105 to 5 × 107. Analysis across each finned HX configuration revealed that a value of 5 × 106 for A mushy provided the closest agreement with experimental observations of the solid-liquid interface evolution and melting fraction.

To ensure the reproducibility of the experimental results, two experiments were repeated under identical conditions. The transient liquid fraction of the tree-shaped finned LHS unit with a circular shell and the Y-shaped finned LHS unit with an obround shell are shown in Fig. 8 . The results show consistent behavior, with negligible differences between two trials. To assess these discrepancies, the root mean square error (RMSE) and the mean absolute error (MAE) were computed [49]. For the tree-shaped finned HX with a circular shell, the RMSE and MAE of the liquid fraction were 1.00 % and 0.82 %, respectively. The obround-shell HX with Y-shaped fins showed RMSE and MAE values of 1.30 % and 0.99 %.

The validity of the presented model was assessed through a comparison of the simulation results with the experimental data. Fig. 9 presents the transient liquid fraction (a global parameter) and the temperature history (a local parameter) obtained from the experimental and numerical data for the finned LHS units across two shell geometries, validating the accuracy of the numerical model, which was quantified through the computation of RMSE and MAE. The results, summarized in Tables 2 and 3
, indicate a strong agreement between the model predictions and the experimental data. The measured discrepancies between experimental and numerical data are likely due to multiple contributing factors. Uncertainties in the physical and thermal properties of the PCM can introduce errors in predicting melting behavior. Moreover, the experimental setup included thermocouples that were not considered in the simulations, which could affect natural convection patterns and alter temperature gradients and flow dynamics [68]. Other contributing factors, such as manufacturing processes including fin cutting, machining, and welding, which can introduce surface roughness and contact thermal resistance, further influence the melting process.

The study evaluates key performance indicators, including the evolution of the melting interface, total melting time, liquid fraction, heat transfer rate, and temperature distribution. Particular attention is given to the performance of Y-shaped and tree-shaped fin configurations, which are compared against a benchmark case employing conventional straight rectangular fins. The analysis aims to elucidate how the integration of bio-inspired geometries and non-circular enclosures can enhance heat transfer in PCM-based systems.

Comparing the evolution of the solid-liquid interface across different LHS units provides valuable insights into the melting behavior of PCM and the dominant heat transfer mechanism during the melting process. Fig. 10 presents the evolution of the solid-liquid interface, streamlines, and velocity field through a combination of experimental observations and numerical simulations for circular-shell LHS units equipped with rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped fins. For comparison, photographs of the rectangular finned HX from our previous study [66] are included. In these photographs, white color denotes the solid phase, and black color represents the liquid phase. The figure demonstrates strong agreement between the experimental observations and numerical predictions. Initially (Fig. 10(a0-c0)), the PCM in direct contact with the heat transfer surfaces begins to melt, exhibiting a near-uniform melting pattern, while the majority of the PCM remains solid. This observation indicates that conduction is the dominant heat transfer mechanism, implying minimal PCM velocities within all LHS units. As long as the viscous forces are strong enough to prevent fluid movement, conduction remains the dominant heat transfer mechanism. During this initial stage, the tree-shaped finned LHS unit exhibits the highest melting rate. This enhanced performance is attributed to the increased heat transfer surface area and the improved heat transfer pathways provided by the tree-shaped fins, which effectively enhance conductive heat transfer while maintaining the identical volume as the other fin configurations. As melting progresses and the thickness of the melting layer increases, the buoyancy force becomes stronger and surpasses the viscous force, resulting in the initiation of the natural convection current. At this stage, the liquid PCM absorbs thermal energy from the heat transfer surfaces, rises due to buoyancy, and is subsequently displaced by cooler liquid PCM descending from the upper regions due to the density difference. The development of buoyancy force induces circulating currents in the upper part of the enclosure, significantly enhancing the melting rate and leading to a substantial increase in velocity magnitude within the LHS units (Fig. 10(a1-c1)). As time elapses, the melt front surface area in the upper part of the enclosure increases, and the size of the vortices in this region grows progressively. This growth results from the increasing strength of the natural convection currents, which significantly influence the propagation of the melting front and the fluid velocity magnitude. Comparing the melting visualizations reveals that tree-shaped fins accelerate the melting rate more effectively than the other configurations during this stage. This enhanced performance is attributed to the combined effect of the increased heat transfer surface area and the redirection of upward convection currents through the increased number of bifurcations, which promotes more uniform heat distribution within the PCM and ultimately accelerates the melting (Fig. 10(a-c) (2 – 4)). Finally, complete melting of the PCM is observed in the upper region of the annulus, while portions of solid PCM remain in the lower section, as shown in Fig. 10(a-c) (4 – 6). This non-uniform melting behavior is attributed to conduction-dominated heat transfer in the lower section of the enclosure, leading to a decreased heat transfer rate and a significant reduction in velocity magnitude. As illustrated in Fig. 10, the presence of the lower fin accelerates the melting rate in the lower region of the HX. Notably, complete melting of the PCM is achieved within 180 min in the tree-shaped finned LHS units, demonstrating their superior heat transfer performance.

To illustrate the influence of fin configuration on PCM melting performance within obround-shell LHS units, Fig. 11 presents the development of the melting front, velocity field, and streamlines for rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped finned LHS units with an obround shell. For comparative purposes, photographs of the rectangular finned HX from our previous research [66] are included. The melting visualizations reveal that the tree-shaped fin configuration outperforms the rectangular and Y-shaped fins in terms of melting rate. As previously mentioned, this superior performance is attributed to the enhanced heat transfer surface area and their ability to deviate convection flows through their bifurcations. Complete melting of the PCM in the tree-shaped finned HX with an obround shell occurs at 105 min, while 91 % and 98 % of the total solid PCM has melted in the rectangular and Y-shaped finned HXs, respectively. For circular-shell HXs, the corresponding melting percentages at 105 min are 87 % for the rectangular, 91 % for the Y-shaped, and 93 % for the tree-shaped finned LHS units. These results highlight that across all fin configurations, the obround shell consistently outperforms the circular shell, with the tree-shaped fins exhibiting the most pronounced melting acceleration in both shell geometries.

Fig. 12 depicts the temporal evolution of the solid-liquid interface in the rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped finned LHS units. This figure facilitates visualization and tracking of the melt interface dynamics within the HXs under investigation. A comparison of the solid-liquid interface development across these configurations reveals that the bio-inspired fin designs (Y-shaped and tree-shaped) significantly accelerate the melt front progression compared to the rectangular fins. Furthermore, the presence of the bottom fin enhances the development of the solid-liquid interface in the lower region of the HX, resulting in enhanced thermal performance.

The transient temperature history recorded by the thermocouples provides critical insights into the thermal response of the PCM throughout melting. Figs. 13 and 14
illustrate the temporal evolution of temperature at four specific locations within finned LHS units with obround and circular shells at a charging temperature of 85
°
C. The recorded temperatures in all LHS units exhibit similar trends, attributable to the heat transfer mechanisms during their respective melting processes. Initially, the temperatures recorded by the thermocouples rise due to conduction heat transfer in the solid PCM. Before reaching the melting temperature, heat transfer to the thermocouples remains predominantly conductive. During this initial stage, the rate of temperature increase is significantly higher for the thermocouples located in the upper part of the HX, indicating a greater rate of heat conduction to the solid PCM in that region. This enhanced heat transfer is due to the circulating convection current within the liquid PCM, which effectively moves heated liquid PCM upwards. As melting progresses and the molten PCM fraction increases, liquid PCM flows over the thermocouple tips, driving further temperature rises. This is attributable to enhanced heat transfer driven by convection currents that transport the heated liquid PCM upwards. During this stage, density gradients within the liquid PCM induce temperature fluctuations at thermocouple T1, resulting in vortex formation and chaotic flow structures. Subsequently, the temperature difference between the liquid PCM and the HTF tube diminishes, and the molten PCM temperature gradually approaches that of the HTF tube through sensible heat absorption. Notably, the thermocouples located in the upper part of the enclosure (T1, T2, and T3) exhibit higher temperatures than those positioned in the lower part (T4). The decreasing temperature trend from the upper to lower thermocouples confirms the presence of rotating flow within the liquid PCM. Thermocouple T4, located at the bottom of the enclosure, shows a different thermal behavior compared to the other three thermocouples, characterized by a nearly linear temperature increase without fluctuations. This observation suggests that heat conduction is the predominant heat transfer mechanism in the lower part of the enclosure, resulting in a slower melting rate in that region.

To illustrate the impact of fin configurations on temperature profiles, Fig. 15 compares the temperature histories at the upper, middle, and bottom locations within rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped finned HXs with obround and circular shells. The findings indicate that the tree-shaped fins exhibit superior heat transfer performance compared to rectangular and Y-shaped fins. The pronounced temperature increase observed at T2 and T3 in the upper part of the tree-shaped finned HX is attributed to the greater fin-PCM contact area and the redirection of convection flows caused by the bifurcated fins, which promote faster heating. However, this distinction diminishes as melting approaches completion, with the temperatures at T2 and T3 in all fin configurations gradually converging to the HTF tube temperature. Moreover, thermocouple T4, in the lower region of the HX, where heat conduction dominates, records a significantly elevated temperature in the tree-shaped finned designs. This increase is due to the enhanced heat conduction facilitated by the tree-shaped fins, thereby accelerating the melting.

Figs. 16 and 17
demonstrate the temperature distributions over time across all LHS units. At the onset of melting, the uniform temperature increase in the PCM near the heat transfer surfaces across all configurations indicates that heat transfer is predominantly conduction-driven. As shown in the figures, the tree-shaped fins improve heat diffusion more effectively than Y-shaped and rectangular fins, which is attributed to their increased heat transfer surface area that effectively enhances conductive heat transfer. As melting progresses, the thermal front propagates outward from the heat transfer surfaces, with a notable upward progression. This phenomenon is attributed to natural convection, driven by buoyancy force resulting from density differences within the molten PCM, which facilitates the mixing of high-temperature PCM in the direction of the natural convection flow. Consequently, these convective currents effectively reduce the temperature gradients and enhance temperature uniformity within the upper region of the enclosure. During this stage, the tree-shaped fins contribute to a more significant temperature rise, consistent with our previous observations. As the melting interface is restricted to the lower half of the HX, the temperature rise rate decreases significantly. This finding corroborates our earlier assertion that conduction is the primary mechanism governing heat transfer in the final melting phase.

To comprehensively characterize the melting behavior within all LHS units, this section analyzes the liquid fraction, total melting time, and melting time reduction across all examined configurations. Fig. 18 illustrates the time progression of the liquid fraction, calculated every five minutes [69], for LHS units with rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped fin configurations, using obround and circular shells. As shown, the liquid fraction in all LHS units initially increases almost linearly and subsequently transitions to a curvilinear trend with a diminishing slope. This transition corresponds to the complete liquefaction of the PCM above the inner tube, which results in a reduction in the strength of natural convection and a change towards conduction as the primary mechanism of heat transfer. Among the configurations examined, the tree-shaped fin design demonstrates the highest melting rate, while the rectangular fin design exhibits the lowest. The superior performance of the tree-shaped configuration is attributed to its larger heat transfer surface area and its enhanced ability to redirect convective flows, both of which are facilitated by increased bifurcation. Over time, the difference in liquid fraction between Y-shaped and tree-shaped finned LHS units diminished.

Fig. 19 presents the complete melting time for rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped finned HXs with obround and circular shells. The rectangular finned LHS units exhibit total melting times of 120 min and 200 min for the obround and circular shells, respectively [66]. Modifying the fin configuration to Y-shaped and tree-shaped designs reduces the total melting time by 8 % and 13 %, respectively, for the obround shell, and 5 % and 10 % for the circular shell. These findings indicate that the tree-shaped finned HX with the obround shell achieves the shortest melting time, while the rectangular finned unit with the circular shell exhibits the longest. Furthermore, altering the shell geometry from circular to obround substantially accelerates the melting rate, reducing total melting time by 40 %, 42 %, and 42 % for the rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped fin configurations, respectively. This is attributed to the increased solid–liquid interface area introduced by the obround shell geometry during the final stage of melting. The obround shell provides a broader surface, resulting in greater contact between the molten and unmelted PCM. This extended interface enhances heat transfer during the slow-melting phase at the lower section of the enclosure, thereby accelerating the melting process. These results suggest that although increasing the bifurcation levels of fins improves melting efficiency, optimizing the shell geometry for each fin configuration is essential to achieving optimal thermal performance.

Fig. 20 illustrates the melting time reduction for all examined LHS units, expressed as a percentage decrease relative to the baseline, which is the rectangular finned circular HX. The rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped finned HXs with the obround shell reduce the melting time by 40 %, 45 %, and 48 %, respectively, relative to the baseline, which indicates that the tree-shaped fin arrangement yields the highest melting time reduction. Conversely, the circular-shell HXs with Y-shaped and tree-shaped fins exhibit significantly lower reductions of 5 % and 10 %, respectively, compared to the baseline. These findings underscore the substantial impact of shell geometry on melting efficiency and highlight the importance of optimizing both fin and shell configurations to maximize heat transfer efficiency.

The variation of the heat transfer rate over time for all investigated configurations is shown in Fig. 21 , highlighting the impact of different heat transfer mechanisms during the melting process. Initially, all configurations exhibit elevated heat transfer rates as a result of direct solid–PCM contact melting, promoting substantial conduction heat transfer. However, the progressive thickening of the liquid PCM layer augments thermal resistance between the solid-liquid interface and heat transfer surfaces, thereby significantly reducing the heat transfer rate in all configurations. Notably, the tree-shaped fin configuration exhibits the highest heat transfer rate during this initial stage, owing to its larger heat transfer surface area. This reduction in heat transfer rate reaches a quasi-steady state in the rectangular finned HX [66], whereas the Y-shaped and tree-shaped finned HXs exhibit a reduced rate of decline. These behaviors arise from the transition from conduction-dominated heat transfer to natural convection as the melting region expands. Moreover, during this stage, the Y-shaped fin configuration exhibits a slightly higher heat transfer rate than the tree-shaped fin configuration, a trend that persists throughout the remaining melting process. After complete melting of the PCM above the inner tube, natural convection decreases, and heat transfer becomes increasingly dominated by conduction, resulting in a decline in the heat transfer rate for all cases.

Fig. 22 compares the time-averaged heat transfer rates for the rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped finned HXs in the obround and circular shell geometries. The tree-shaped fin configuration exhibits the highest time-averaged heat transfer rate, with improvements of 12 % in the obround shell and 10 % in the circular shell compared to their corresponding rectangular finned HXs. The Y-shaped finned HXs also show enhancements of 6 % and 7 % for the obround and circular shells, respectively. Across all fin configurations, the obround shell consistently demonstrates higher time-averaged heat transfer rates relative to the circular shell, with improvements of 58 %, 55 %, and 61 % observed for the rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped finned HXs, respectively.

Fig. 23 presents the time-averaged heat transfer rate enhancement for all studied cases, defined as the percentage increase relative to the baseline, which is the rectangular finned HX with a circular shell. Across all fin configurations, HXs with the obround shell consistently outperform the baseline, with a maximum enhancement of 77 % observed in the tree-shaped finned HX. In addition, the rectangular and Y-shaped finned HXs with the obround shell show enhancements of 58 % and 66 %, respectively.

Fig. 24 illustrates the variation of absorbed energy over time for rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped finned HXs with obround and circular shells. It is observed that during the initial stage (t ≤ 10 min), the absorbed energy exhibits a linear increase with a high slope across all LHS units, indicating a significant temperature difference between the heat transfer surfaces and the average PCM temperature. As melting progresses, absorbed energy continues to rise, which can be attributed to the enhanced melting rate in the upper half of the enclosure due to the convection-dominated heat transfer in that region, leading to a higher rate of latent heat absorption. At the last stage of the melting process, the rate of increase gradually slows down as the intensity of natural convection diminishes and the temperature gradient decreases.

To enhance heat transfer performance in LHS units during the melting process, a rigorous evaluation of fin structural parameters is crucial. Fin length and thickness have been identified as primary factors influencing the overall efficiency of finned HXs. Therefore, this section presents a parametric study investigating the effects of fin length and thickness on the PCM melting rate. The analysis was conducted for both circular and obround HXs with rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped fin configurations. In this study, fin thickness was reduced while fin length was increased, subject to geometric constraints imposed by the shell dimensions. In all configurations, a minimum gap of 1 mm was maintained between the fin tips and the inner shell surface to prevent the obstruction of convection currents moving from the lower to the upper part of the HX. The detailed fin geometries, including fin length (L) and thickness (W), employed in the analysis are presented in Tables 4 and 5
, where b₁, b₂, and b₃ represent the characteristic dimensions of branches 1, 2, and 3 for each fin configuration. As shown, progressing from Case 1 to Case 3 corresponds to an increase in fin length and a decrease in fin thickness, while maintaining an equal total fin volume across all designs. Figs. 25 and 26
illustrate the temporal evolution of liquid fraction for various fin thicknesses in rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped finned HXs with circular and obround shells. The results clearly demonstrate that across all configurations, reducing fin thickness accelerates PCM melting. This improvement is attributed to the corresponding increase in effective heat transfer surface area and heat penetration depth. Fig. 27 further supports these findings by comparing the melting times in circular and obround HXs incorporating rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped fins across different fin thicknesses. As observed, decreasing fin thickness reduces melting time in both shell geometries, with the effect being most pronounced for tree-shaped fin configurations. Specifically, reducing fin thickness to case 3 results in 74 % and 41 % melting time reductions in tree-shaped finned HXs with circular and obround shells, respectively. In comparison, rectangular and Y-shaped fins show melting time reductions of 54 % and 61 % in the circular shell, and 27 % and 32 % in the obround shell, respectively. Furthermore, across all fin configurations, the benefit of reducing fin thickness is consistently less pronounced in the obround shell compared to the circular shell.

This study investigated the influence of fin configuration and shell geometry on the melting performance of paraffin as a PCM within shell-and-tube thermal storage units. Experimental and numerical analyses were conducted on transparent LHS heat exchangers featuring rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped fin designs integrated into circular and obround enclosures. All configurations maintained identical volumes of PCM and fins to enable a fair comparison. The results provided comprehensive insights into melting front progression, liquid fraction evolution, transient heat transfer rate, temperature history, and total melting time. Key findings are summarized as follows:
•
Modifying the fin configuration from rectangular to Y-shaped and tree-shaped reduced the melting time by 8 % and 13 % in the obround shells and by 5 % and 10 % in the circular shells, respectively. These findings highlight a synergistic interaction between the bio-inspired tree-shaped fins and the obround geometry, which enhanced the heat transfer rate and resulted in the shortest melting time.
•
The tree-shaped fin configuration achieved the highest time-averaged heat transfer rate, improving by 12 % in the obround shell and 10 % in the circular shell relative to rectangular finned HXs. The Y-shaped fin configuration also enhanced heat transfer, showing improvements of 6 % and 7 % for the obround and circular shells, respectively.
•
Modifying the shell configuration from circular to obround significantly decreased total melting time by 40 %, 42 %, and 42 % for the rectangular, Y-shaped, and tree-shaped fin configurations, respectively. This modification also substantially enhanced the time-averaged heat transfer rate by 58 %, 55 %, and 61 % across these fin configurations. These findings underscored the significant advantage of the obround shell geometry in accelerating heat transfer and shortening total melting time.
•
Relative to the baseline, rectangular finned circular HX, the tree-shaped finned HX with an obround shell demonstrated the most significant time-averaged heat transfer rate enhancement, achieving a 77 % improvement. This configuration also resulted in the greatest reduction in melting time, with a decrease of 48 %. Rectangular and Y-shaped finned HXs with obround shell also provided substantial benefits, enhancing heat transfer rates by 58 % and 66 %, respectively, and reducing melting time by 40 % and 45 %.
•
Reducing fin thickness, while maintaining a constant total fin volume, accelerated the melting rate in both shell geometries across all fin configurations, with the effect being most pronounced for tree-shaped fin designs. Specifically, for the tree-shaped fin configuration, the smallest fin thickness (case 3) led to melting time reductions of 74 % in the circular shell and 41 % in the obround shell.
